text: This is Logan Mellert with National Surgery Review. Thank you for joining us today as we walk you through a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon accesses the abdomen at Palmer's point in the left upper quadrant using an optical trocar, providing a clear view of the entry site.
A 5mm trocar is inserted at the umbilicus, and two 3mm trocars are placed along the right costal margin, establishing access points for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Atraumatic graspers are used to grasp the gallbladder and retract it cephalad and laterally, exposing the triangle of Calot.
The laparoscopic view shows the initial inspection of the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins careful dissection using an L-hook electrocautery device, focusing on precise tissue separation within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses an instrument to carefully hook and apply anterior tension to adhesions, preventing inadvertent injury to the duodenum or ductal structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the perineal attachments located behind the gallbladder, using precise instrument manipulation to separate these structures.
The surgical team pauses to visually identify the anatomical structures within the exposed triangle of Calot, including the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct.

The surgeon visually identifies the anatomical boundaries of the triangle of Calot, defined by the common hepatic duct medially, the cystic duct laterally, and the inferior edge of the liver superiorly.
The surgeon continues dissection within the triangle of Calot, allowing for the identification of the cystic artery amidst the surrounding anatomical structures.
Although this region is classically defined as a triangle, it is important to remember that this is a three-dimensional space and tends to take the form of a rhomboid.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery on the wall of the gallbladder, using it as a guide for dissection within the anatomical region defined by the triangle of Calot.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect the cystic duct, separating it from surrounding tissue within the anatomical region defined by the triangle of Calot.
The surgeon dissects attachments from both the anterior and posterior aspects of the cystic duct, aiding in the clear delineation of the duct within the surgical field.
The surgeon shifts focus to the cystic artery after fully dissecting the cystic duct, preparing to isolate and manage the artery within the rhomboid-shaped anatomical space.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety by clearing the hepatocystic triangle of fat and fibrinous tissues, revealing only the cystic duct and artery entering the gallbladder, with the lower third of the cystic plate exposed.
The surgeon attempts to identify the common bile duct and common hepatic duct prior to ligating the cystic duct, ensuring these structures are visually distinguished despite not being exposed.
If there is ambiguity with anatomy, a cholangiogram should be performed to delineate the structures.
The surgeon uses plastic clips to ligate the cystic duct after confirming the anatomy, ensuring secure closure of the duct.
The surgeon places two plastic clips proximally and one clip distally on the cystic artery, securing it after the dissection.
The surgeon uses hook scissors to divide the cystic duct, following its ligation with plastic clips, as part of the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses hook scissors to carefully divide the cystic duct, minimizing the risk of inadvertent injury during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies two clips to the cystic artery and uses electrocautery to divide the artery distally, ensuring hemostasis.
The surgeon identifies a small posterior cystic artery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon manages a branch similar to the cystic artery by placing one clip proximally and using electrocautery to divide it distally.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully separating the gallbladder from the surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to carefully dissect within the areolar plane between the gallbladder and the liver bed, aiming to separate the gallbladder while minimizing tissue damage.
The surgeon performs a delicate dissection between the gallbladder and liver bed, carefully maintaining the correct depth to avoid liver bed bleeding or gallbladder entry and spillage.
The surgeon identifies and controls liver bed bleeding using electrocautery, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
In patients with acute cholecystitis, the areolar plane between the gallbladder and liver bed is more clearly delineated due to visible edema in the tissue.
The gallbladder, having been dissected from the liver bed, is grasped at the cystic duct and pulled through the trocar for removal from the abdomen.
The surgeon pulls the gallbladder out of the abdomen without using a retrieval bag, as there is no significant gallbladder pathology or cholelithiasis present.
The surgeon reinspects the liver bed after gallbladder removal, checking for hemostasis before proceeding with desufflation.
Until next time, this is Logan Miller with National Surgery Review.
Thank you.
